teamContext: Solo reflection day — no specific team
objective: Consolidate Week 1 learnings, identify your strongest and weakest areas, and set a clear intention for Week 2.

realWorldAnchor.example: Connecting the dots: CRED (personas), Meesho (user research), Zepto (JTBD), PhonePe (metrics), Razorpay (prioritisation), Zomato (PRDs)

tasks.title: The Weekly Review — A PM Habit Worth Building
tasks.summary: Top PMs treat weekly reflection as a product habit. This piece explains how to run a personal weekly review that surfaces patterns, gaps, and improvement areas.
keyTakeaways: What got done vs what moved the needle are different questions
Capture learnings in writing — your future self will thank you
The best PMs know their own blind spots through reflection, not feedback alone
A 30-minute weekly review compounds into a massive learning advantage over 6 months

tasks.keyword: APM reflection learning journal product management growth
tasks.whyItMatters: Week 2 gets harder. Knowing where you struggled in Week 1 helps you prepare for concepts that build on those foundations.
whatToLearn: How experienced PMs reflect on their own growth
Common Week 1 struggles for APM candidates
How to turn assessment feedback into a personal learning system
Any frameworks for tracking PM skill development over time

tasks.title: Week 1 Reflection Session
tasks.promptTitle: Week 1 Reflection with Your PM Coach
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM coach running a Week 1 end-of-week reflection session with Pranav.

He has completed 6 days covering: PM role, user research, JTBD + personas, metrics, prioritisation, and PRDs.

Ask him these questions one at a time:
1. 'Which of the 6 days felt most natural to you? Why?'
2. 'Which day felt most difficult or confusing?'
3. 'What is one thing from Week 1 you could apply to a real product decision today?'
4. 'If you had to explain JTBD to a friend in 60 seconds, what would you say?'
5. 'What do you think Week 2 (working with engineering, design, and data) will challenge you on?'

After his responses, give him:
- His Week 1 strengths (based on his reflections)
- His Week 1 development area
- One mental model to carry into Week 2
- A week-end score: how ready is he for Week 2? (1-10)

Be encouraging but honest.

bonusTasks.title: PhonePe's Mobile-First Approach to 500M Users
bonusTasks.summary: PhonePe was designed for Bharat — low-end Android devices, patchy networks, first-time smartphone users. Their mobile decisions shaped India's UPI adoption.
keyTakeaways: Performance IS a feature when your users are on 2G
Offline-first design isn't optional in Tier-2 India
Localisation goes beyond language — it includes flow and mental models

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Week 1 capstone — your PM philosophy statement
assessmentTask.rawMaterial: You have now covered: the PM role, user research, JTBD, personas, metrics, prioritisation, and PRDs. You've seen examples from Zepto, CRED, Meesho, PhonePe, Razorpay, and Zomato.
assessmentTask.outputFormat:
Write a personal PM philosophy statement (150-200 words) that answers:
1. What do you believe is the most important thing a PM can do?
2. What do you think most PMs get wrong?
3. What kind of PM do you want to be in 2 years?
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are reviewing Pranav's Week 1 capstone — his personal PM philosophy statement.

This is a reflective piece, not a technical exercise. Evaluate:

**What I Got Right** — clarity of thinking, honesty, specificity
**What Needs Correction** — clichés, generic statements, anything that sounds borrowed rather than believed
**Blind Spots** — gaps in his self-awareness based on his Week 1 performance
**Growth Signal** — one thing this statement reveals about his potential as a PM
**Intent for Week 2** — one specific thing to focus on

Score: X/10
Reforge Level: Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform
